Source_Species.reason: The study uses rice plants; OsmiR166b is a rice miRNA.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Rice is explicitly given as Oryza sativa L. in the paper.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: All transgenic plants were generated from Zhonghua 11 (ZH11).

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Applied species is Oryza sativa L. according to Methods.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Applied variety is Zhonghua 11 (japonica cv. ZH11).

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: OsmiR166b is a microRNA and the paper states miR166 forms a big family in rice.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results directly show OsmiR166b manipulation changes cellulose and lignin contents in leaves and culms.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Cellulose is a polysaccharide and lignin is discussed as part of the phenylpropanoid/monolignol-derived cell wall pathway; this is consistent with the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Histochemical staining and microscopy assess cellulose/lignin deposition in leaf and culm cell walls, especially around vascular bundles and supporting tissues; sclerenchyma under epidermis is also shown.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper repeatedly links cellulose/lignin deposition and wall thickness to mechanical strength of rice tissues.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: OE166b and RNAiHox32 increase cellulose/lignin and wall thickness; STTM166b and OEHox32 reduce them and show brittle culms. OsmiR166b represses OsHox32 by cleavage.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are reported: STTM, overexpression, qRT-PCR, target-site mutation/RT-PCR, cellulose/lignin measurement, histochemical staining, SEM, and TEM.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Discussion states OsmiR166b was previously reported to be differentially expressed in different tissues; this paper does not detail the pattern further.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Gene expression analyses were performed mainly using leaves collected at tillering stage in transgenic rice.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Plants were grown in a paddy field under natural conditions during summer and autumn seasons.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: OsmiR166b is shown to cleave OsHox32 transcript, but the paper does not present protein-protein interaction for a miRNA with OsHox32.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summarized findings are directly supported by Results.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods state OsMIR166b overexpression used the 35S promoter.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Potential use for improving culm strength/lodging resistance is a reasonable implication, but this application value is not explicitly stated in the paper.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Reduced miR166b activity (STTM166b) caused rolled/drooping leaves, shorter internodes, reduced plant height, and brittle culms; broader HD-ZIP III statement is inferential but consistent.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper is about mechanical strength/cell wall composition, not nutritional quality; the phrase about 'nutritional quality' is not supported by the article.

corrections.reason: OsmiR166b is shown to cleave OsHox32 transcript, but the paper does not present protein-protein interaction for a miRNA with OsHox32.

corrections.old_value: Although central phenotype is mechanical strength rather than nutritional quality, the metabolite-relevant outputs are cellulose and lignin deposition in cell walls
corrections.reason: The paper is about mechanical strength/cell wall composition, not nutritional quality; the phrase about 'nutritional quality' is not supported by the article.

Source_Species.reason: OsCAD2 is a rice gene discussed in rice transgenic plants.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Rice is Oryza sativa L. in the paper.

Applied_Species.reason: Assays are performed in rice and in some reporter/protoplast systems, but the gene context is rice.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Applied rice species is Oryza sativa.

Enzyme_Name.reason: OsCAD2 is explicitly referred to as a CAD gene; the name cinnamyl alcohol dehydrogenase 2 is directly implied by gene symbol and text.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number is provided in the paper.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: OsCAD2 is identified as a CAD gene; CAD family assignment is supported, but no specific domain architecture is given.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The paper explicitly places OsCAD2 in lignin/monolignol biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Text states CAD genes function in the last step of monolignol biosynthesis.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: Described as the last step of monolignol biosynthesis, i.e. downstream/terminal.

Primary_Substrate.reason: The paper states CAD catalyzes hydroxy-cinnamyl aldehydes into corresponding alcohols.

Primary_Product.reason: The corresponding alcohols are explicitly mentioned, i.e. monolignols.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This reaction description is stated almost verbatim in Results/Discussion.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: OsCAD2 is described as acting in the last step of monolignol biosynthesis, directly tied to lignin formation.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper says OsCAD2 is the bona fide CAD gene playing the major role and discusses it as a key switch in Discussion, but does not experimentally establish classic rate-limiting status.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: OsCAD2 is directly linked to lignin biosynthesis and lignin content.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Lignin is the relevant terminal polymer discussed.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Lignin deposition is examined in leaf and culm cell walls, especially vascular bundle/supporting tissues.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper states lignin gives wall rigidity and resistance to compressive force, contributing to mechanical strength.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Higher OsCAD2 expression correlates with higher lignin in OE166b/RNAiHox32; repression by OsHox32 is linked to lower lignin in STTM166b/OEHox32.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: qRT-PCR, Y1H, ChIP-PCR, and dual-luciferase are all used regarding OsCAD2 regulation.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Methods state expression was measured in leaves at tillering stage; Results show increased expression in OE166b/RNAiHox32 and decreased in STTM166b/OEHox32.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The paper does not report subcellular localization of OsCAD2.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Plants were grown in paddy field under natural conditions.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: OsCAD2 is shown as a transcriptional target of OsHox32 and OSH15, not as a protein shown to physically interact with them.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three statements are supported by qRT-PCR, promoter binding assays, and dual-luciferase assays.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Dual-luciferase assay used the native OsCAD2 promoter region (2500 bp upstream of ATG) as reporter.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a plausible implication from the findings, but the paper does not explicitly frame OsCAD2 as an engineering target.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Reduced OsCAD2 expression is linked in the paper's regulatory model and cited literature to lower lignin and weaker mechanical support.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper explicitly says OsCAD2 is the bona fide/major CAD in rice culm lignification/monolignol biosynthesis.

corrections.reason: No EC number is provided in the paper.
The paper does not report subcellular localization of OsCAD2.
OsCAD2 is shown as a transcriptional target of OsHox32 and OSH15, not as a protein shown to physically interact with them.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Results explicitly identify OsHox32 as Loc_Os03g43930.

Source_Species.reason: OsHox32 is a rice gene used in rice transgenic experiments.

Regulator_Type.reason: OsHox32 is explicitly treated as a transcription factor.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper states OsHox32 is a START domain-containing gene and Discussion identifies it as an HD-ZIP III family gene.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The paper does not directly determine or state nuclear localization of OsHox32.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: OsHox32 manipulation alters cellulose and lignin contents in leaves and culms.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Cellulose and lignin classes are consistent with the metabolites analyzed in the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Cell wall deposition in leaves and culms is directly measured and visualized.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Cellulose/lignin-linked wall support and mechanical strength are central findings.

Metabolic_Process_Controlled.reason: Summary and Results state OsmiR166b-OsHox32 regulates genes related to MLG, cellulose and lignin biosynthesis.

Regulation_Mode.reason: OsHox32 directly suppresses OsCAD2 and OsCESA7 and co-suppresses OsCAD2 with OSH15.

Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes.reason: Direct repression of OsCAD2 and OsCESA7 is shown; altered expression of OsCESA6, OsCSLF6 and Os4CL3 is observed without direct binding evidence.

Primary_Regulatory_Targets.reason: OsCAD2 and OsCESA7 are directly bound by OsHox32 in Y1H/ChIP assays.

Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs.reason: OsmiR166b cleavage of OsHox32 is experimentally confirmed.

Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product.reason: Overexpression lowers cellulose/lignin and weakens walls; suppression has opposite effects, supporting major negative regulatory influence.

Protein_Complex_Involvement.reason: Physical interaction with OSH15 and synergistic repression of OsCAD2 are shown.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: OSH15 is a true interacting protein by Y2H/BiFC, but OsmiR166b is not a protein; listing it under interacting proteins is biologically inaccurate even though it targets OsHox32 transcript.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four listed findings are directly supported by the Results.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: OEHox32 reduces cellulose/lignin and mechanical strength; RNAiHox32 has opposite trends.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are reported in the paper.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Expression was manipulated and assayed mainly in leaves at tillering stage, with phenotypes in leaves and culms.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Plants were grown in paddy field under natural conditions.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods state overexpression used 35S promoter and suppression used an RNAi construct.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is an inference from the data; the paper does not explicitly present OsHox32 downregulation as a breeding strategy.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Overexpression causes brittle culms, drooping/rolled leaves, shorter internodes and reduced plant height.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper explicitly states the START domain harbors the miR166b cleavage site and is required for interaction with OSH15.

corrections.reason: The paper does not directly determine or state nuclear localization of OsHox32.

Source_Species.reason: OSH15 is discussed as a rice gene and tested in rice-related assays/protoplasts.

Applied_Species.reason: OSH15 is studied in the rice gene regulatory context.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Applied species context is Oryza sativa.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The paper uses ZH11 generally, but OSH15 itself was not manipulated as a transgene in ZH11 plants; assays include Arabidopsis protoplasts and yeast.

Regulator_Type.reason: OSH15 is explicitly described as a Class I KNOX/homeobox transcription factor from prior work and used as a regulator in this study.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper states OSH15 is a Class I KNOX protein and mentions its KNOX, ELK and HD domains.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: No direct localization of OSH15 is reported in this paper.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: In Results, OSH15 is linked to repression of OsCAD2 and thus lignin biosynthesis.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Lignin is the metabolite class endpoint discussed for OSH15-related regulation.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The paper/cited work links OSH15 to internode lignification and this study tests OsCAD2 repression, but it does not directly measure OSH15-dependent lignin deposition in leaves and culms here.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Lignin function in wall rigidity and mechanical strength is discussed in the study.

Metabolic_Process_Controlled.reason: OSH15 is presented as a negative regulator of lignin biosynthesis via OsCAD2.

Regulation_Mode.reason: It suppresses OsCAD2 and shows co-repression with OsHox32 in dual-luciferase assays.

Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes.reason: The paper states OSH15 suppresses OsCAD2 and synergistically enhances OsHox32-mediated repression of OsCAD2.

Primary_Regulatory_Targets.reason: OsCAD2 is the specific target discussed.

Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product.reason: OSH15 is shown as an interacting co-repressor, but its 'decisive' importance is not directly tested in this paper because OSH15 was not genetically manipulated here.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: OsHox32 interaction is shown by Y2H and BiFC.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three statements are supported by the Results.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: This study does not directly test OSH15 phenotypes; the statement is inferred from regulatory assays and prior literature.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Y2H, BiFC and dual-luciferase are exactly the methods used for OSH15 in this paper.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is speculative and not explicitly proposed in the paper.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Likely consequence is inferable from lignin reduction, but the paper does not directly test overactive OSH15 in this study.

Other_Important_Info.reason: OSH15 is indeed experimentally interrogated in Results as an interacting co-regulator affecting OsCAD2 repression.

corrections.reason: No direct localization of OSH15 is reported in this paper.
